subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s degree, earned at age 18
Completed major coursework in accounting, finance, and taxation
Participated in tax preparation workshops and seminars during studies
Has completed annual IRS continuing education for tax preparers
Received on-the-job training in tax software and bookkeeping tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Tax Preparer
Prepares tax returns for both individual and business clients
Also serves as a bookkeeper and financial document preparer
Provides tax advice and planning strategies to clients
Responsible for e-filing returns and preparing supporting reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the finance and accounting sector
Specializes in tax preparation and bookkeeping services
Serves clients across various industries, primarily small businesses and individuals

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works in a small to medium-sized accounting firm
Firm has fewer than 50 employees
Collaborates closely with a small team of fellow tax preparers and bookkeepers
The environment is client-focused and deadline-driven

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Firm primarily serves small businesses and individual taxpayers
Handles both consumer (personal) and business tax preparation
Focuses on year-round bookkeeping and tax advisory services
Market segment includes sole proprietors, LLCs, and contract workers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 3-5 years of experience as a tax preparer
Began working in the field immediately after completing degree
Experience includes multiple tax seasons and annual bookkeeping cycles

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Considered early-career or junior to mid-level in the field
Handles independent client portfolios but not in a management role
Regularly consults with senior preparers on complex cases
Trusted with significant client-facing responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Performs monthly and annual bookkeeping for clients
Prepares and files individual and business tax returns
Creates financial documents such as profit & loss (P&L) statements and contract labor reports
Issues 1099 forms and manages related reporting
Reviews client tax documents and provides advice on tax strategies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Bookkeeping and financial report creation: approximately 50% of work hours
Tax preparation and filing: around 30% of time, especially during tax season
Client communication and advisory: roughly 10-15% of time
Administrative and research tasks: about 5-10% of work hours

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates with moderate autonomy in managing client accounts
Makes independent decisions on tax deduction strategies and bookkeeping methods
Refers highly complex or unusual tax situations to more senior colleagues
Responsible for the accuracy and completeness of client filings
Authorized to communicate directly with clients on most issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Running standard bookkeeping tasks in QuickBooks
Reconciling accounts and categorizing common expenses
Generating routine P&L reports and contract labor summaries
Using Excel for basic financial calculations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Handling complex tax situations, such as multi-state filings or unusual deductions
Interpreting new or ambiguous IRS regulations
Correcting significant bookkeeping errors from client data
Advising clients on advanced tax reduction strategies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Monthly transaction categorization in bookkeeping
Data entry of receipts and invoices into QuickBooks
Generating and issuing standard 1099 forms
Compiling year-end reports and tax summaries for clients

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys providing tax advice and helping clients reduce liability
Finds satisfaction in solving complex tax challenges
Considers repetitive data entry and monthly reconciliations tedious
Prefers client-facing communications over back-end administrative work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data entry from client documents can be time-consuming and error-prone
Integrating data between QuickBooks, Excel, and TaxWise sometimes requires double work
Clients providing incomplete or disorganized documentation delays progress
Occasional software glitches, especially during peak tax season, disrupt workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Finds that enjoyment and productivity are highest when solving complex client problems
Skill level is highest in routine bookkeeping, leading to faster task completion
Least productive when dealing with tedious, repetitive data entry
Enjoyment increases when learning new strategies or tools that improve workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Has a strong interest in adopting new tech that improves efficiency
Regularly explores software updates and new features
Views technology as an essential part of modern tax preparation
Positive about experimenting with AI and automation tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to updated versions of QuickBooks and TaxWise
Comfortable switching between desktop and online platforms
Frequently seeks out online tutorials or peer advice when learning new tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Invests regular time in learning about software updates and features
Participates in webinars or online courses on new tax software
Believes time spent on tech training is worthwhile if it increases productivity
Sometimes faces time constraints during peak periods that limit deep-dive training

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the need to reduce repetitive work and errors
Barriers include heavy workloads during tax season
More likely to adopt tech when it integrates with existing tools
Requires clear evidence of time-saving or accuracy benefits before fully committing

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share tips on maximizing software efficiency
Firm management encourages adoption of new tools that streamline workflow
More likely to adopt tools that are recommended by experienced colleagues
Team discussions help evaluate the value of new technologies before implementation

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses QuickBooks Desktop and QuickBooks Online for bookkeeping
Relies on TaxWise for tax preparation and e-filing
Regularly uses Excel for manual reporting and data analysis
Accesses IRS.gov for up-to-date tax guidance and forms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Excel spreadsheets for custom financial reports
Physical document scanners for digitizing client paperwork
Email for client communication and document exchange
Printer and secure file storage for signed tax returns

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient with QuickBooks, considered an industry staple for bookkeeping
Comfortable navigating multiple tax software platforms, including TaxWise
Has experience with e-filing systems and secure document portals
Familiar with government resources such as the IRS website and 'Where’s My Refund'

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally encounters data compatibility issues moving between QuickBooks and Excel
Manual data transfer sometimes required between bookkeeping and tax prep platforms
Limited integration between TaxWise and client document management systems
Relies on exporting/importing CSV files to bridge software gaps

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Uses large language models (LLMs) regularly, understands their basic operation
Familiar with concepts like natural language processing and text generation
Aware of AI's potential for automating routine communication and document drafting
Understands limitations of AI in domain-specific decision-making

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT and other LLM-based assistants for drafting emails and letters
Occasionally uses AI-powered grammar and style tools (e.g., Grammarly)
Explores AI features embedded in office productivity suites

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies AI tools weekly for drafting client correspondence
Uses LLMs to rephrase or clarify complex tax information in emails
Employs AI to generate templates for standard client letters
Occasionally uses AI for brainstorming or outlining tax advice communications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used LLMs to draft clear, professional client letters, saving significant time
Encountered occasional inaccuracies when AI generated tax-related explanations, requiring manual review
Noticed that AI-drafted content sometimes lacked specific tax terminology
Learned the importance of reviewing AI-generated content for compliance and accuracy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Firm provides informal guidance and best practices for AI tool use
Peers share tips and templates for integrating AI-drafted content into workflow
No formal AI training program, but self-directed learning is encouraged
Occasional team discussions on ethical considerations of AI-generated communications

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers using AI tools independently for drafting and revising communications
Occasionally uses AI in a step-by-step manner for complex tasks like outlining letters
Finds flexibility in switching between independent and guided AI interactions
Appreciates being able to iterate on AI outputs before finalizing

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an assistant for drafting and organizing written content
Treats AI as a tool to enhance, not replace, professional expertise
Uses AI as a brainstorming partner for improving client communication
Maintains final editorial control over all AI-generated output

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to integrating AI into more aspects of workflow if it saves time
Actively seeks out new AI features in office and tax software
Willing to test AI-driven automation for repetitive reporting tasks
Prefers gradual adoption, starting with low-risk communication tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational AI interfaces for drafting and revising text
Finds natural language prompts intuitive and easy to use
Uses command-based features for quick formatting or data extraction tasks
Likes the option to switch between conversational and command modes as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many tasks, like tax planning and advisory, depend heavily on detailed domain knowledge
Bookkeeping and tax preparation require understanding of nuanced IRS regulations
AI is currently used only for supporting tasks, not for making tax decisions
Reviews all AI outputs for accuracy and compliance with tax laws

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for drafting communications but not for making final tax decisions
Always double-checks AI-generated information for technical accuracy
Considers AI a useful starting point, not an authoritative source
More likely to trust AI with routine, low-risk writing tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Favors high automation for repetitive document drafting and formatting
Prefers human oversight for all client-facing advice and tax filing
Supports a collaborative approach: AI drafts, human reviews and finalizes
Envisions increased automation in data entry but not in tax judgment calls

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for standardized, low-risk processes like letter templates
Finds automation threatening if it bypasses required compliance checks
Worried about automation in tasks that require professional judgment or legal liability
Requires clear override and review mechanisms before trusting full automation

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances productivity but increases the need for critical review skills
Feels that communication and domain expertise remain essential
Sees AI as expanding rather than replacing core competencies
Values the ability to interpret and contextualize AI outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning prompt engineering to get better results from LLMs
Developing skills in reviewing and editing AI-generated content
Responsible for ensuring AI compliance with regulatory and ethical standards
Exploring new ways to automate routine report generation using AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Low concern about AI replacing core tax advisory work
Mild concern about automation reducing demand for entry-level data entry tasks
Believes the profession will shift towards more advisory and oversight functions
Sees AI as augmenting, not threatening, current job responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews and edits AI-generated client communications before sending
Manual checks are built into workflow for all AI-assisted tasks
No AI-driven process is fully automated without human review
Firm policy requires human sign-off on all final tax documents

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Firm is gradually updating policies to include guidelines for responsible AI use
Team discussions focus on balancing efficiency with compliance and ethics
No formal policy yet, but growing emphasis on AI as a productivity tool
Encouraged to share AI-related best practices within the team

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will make tax preparation more efficient and accessible
Sees potential for AI to reduce manual errors in finance and accounting
Cautious about AI’s impact on job displacement in repetitive roles
Thinks AI may increase demand for higher-level analytical skills

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes AI must be used transparently, especially in client communication
Concerned about data privacy and the potential for AI-generated errors
Supports strong human oversight to prevent unethical outcomes
Values clear guidelines for responsible AI use in financial services

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from AI that can accurately categorize complex transactions in QuickBooks
Wants an AI assistant that can automatically compile and summarize tax document packages
Sees value in AI-powered reconciliation tools for error detection
Desires deeper integration between tax software and bookkeeping platforms with AI support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine data entry and report generation to become largely automated
Anticipates increased focus on client advisory and interpretation of AI-generated insights
Sees continued need for human expertise in complex tax and compliance scenarios
Believes AI will accelerate the pace and accuracy of tax preparation work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Develop formal training programs for responsible AI tool use
Invest in AI solutions that integrate with current accounting platforms
Establish clear guidelines for human oversight of AI-driven processes
Encourage staff to share experiences and best practices for AI adoption
